schools.summary: Joseph bringing his sons to the sick Jacob: 'and it came to pass after these things, that one told Joseph, Behold, thy father is sick: and he took with him his two sons, Manasseh and Ephraim' — Joseph informed of Jacob's illness (the aged patriarch near death, 47:28-31, the summons to the dying father, the occasion of the blessing, vv.5-20, the setting of Jacob's deathbed); Joseph taking his two sons (Manasseh and Ephraim, born in Egypt, 41:50-52) to his father (the desire that they be blessed by the dying patriarch, the bringing of the sons for the blessing, and the adoption, v.5); Joseph's bringing his sons to be blessed — the faith that valued the patriarch's blessing (the covenant blessing) above all the honors of Egypt (the desire that his sons share the covenant, not the Egyptian inheritance only, the setting of the great deathbed scene, Jacob's faith, Hebrews 11:21) — Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And it came to pass after these things, that one told Joseph, Behold, thy father is sick: and he took with him his two sons, Manasseh and Ephraim
Joseph bringing his sons to the sick Jacob
the dying patriarch and the occasion of the blessing (47:28; 41:50)
the faith that valued the covenant blessing above all Egypt's honors (Hebrews 11:21)

schools.summary: Jacob rousing himself for Joseph's visit: 'and one told Jacob, and said, Behold, thy son Joseph cometh unto thee: and Israel strengthened himself, and sat upon the bed' — Jacob, told of Joseph's coming, gathering his failing strength and sitting up on the bed (the aged patriarch rousing himself for the solemn act, the blessing, the effort of the dying man, the dignity of the occasion); Jacob 'strengthened himself' (gathered his strength) — the dying patriarch making the effort to sit up (the resolve for the blessing, the failing body roused for the solemn act); 'sat upon the bed' (Jacob sitting up, the posture of the blessing, the solemn act, the patriarch composing himself, the dignity of the deathbed scene, the blessing of faith, Hebrews 11:21) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And one told Jacob, and said, Behold, thy son Joseph cometh unto thee: and Israel strengthened himself, and sat upon the bed
Jacob rousing himself for Joseph's visit
the dying patriarch gathering his strength for the solemn blessing
the dignity of the deathbed scene (Hebrews 11:21)

schools.summary: Jacob recalling God's appearing at Luz: 'and Jacob said unto Joseph, God Almighty appeared unto me at Luz in the land of Canaan, and blessed me' — Jacob recalling God's appearing to him at Luz (Bethel, 28:19; 35:6-12) — the vision of the ladder and the covenant promise (the foundation of all that followed, the patriarch rehearsing the covenant before blessing the lads); 'God Almighty' (El Shaddai, the name of covenant power and promise, 17:1; 35:11) — the God who had appeared and blessed (the almighty covenant God, the source of the blessing Jacob would now convey); 'appeared unto me at Luz... and blessed me' (the appearing at Bethel, 28:13-15; 35:9-12, where God blessed Jacob and renewed the covenant, the ground of Jacob's authority to bless and adopt, vv.4-5, the covenant rehearsed, to convey it to Joseph's sons) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Jacob said unto Joseph, God Almighty appeared unto me at Luz in the land of Canaan, and blessed me
Jacob recalling God's appearing at Luz
the vision at Bethel and the covenant promise (28:19; 35:6-12)
God Almighty (El Shaddai), the ground of the blessing Jacob would convey

schools.summary: Jacob rehearsing the covenant promise: 'and said unto me, Behold, I will make thee fruitful, and multiply thee, and I will make of thee a multitude of people; and will give this land to thy seed after thee for an everlasting possession' — Jacob rehearsing the covenant promise (35:11): fruitfulness, multiplication, a multitude of people (the nation, indeed 'a company of nations,' the promise of the seed, the covenant of the patriarchs, 12:2; 17:4-6); 'a multitude of people' (a company of nations, 35:11) — the promise of the numerous seed (Israel, and indeed the tribes, the covenant multiplication, the ground of the adoption, Ephraim a multitude of nations, v.19); 'will give this land to thy seed after thee for an everlasting possession' (the promise of Canaan, the everlasting possession, 17:8, the covenant land, to which God would bring them again, v.21, the ground of the inheritance Jacob conveyed, the lads sharing the covenant land, not the Egyptian, the providence, the covenant secured) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, JFB.

themes: And said unto me, Behold, I will make thee fruitful, and multiply thee... and will give this land to thy seed after thee for an everlasting possession
Jacob rehearsing the covenant promise
the covenant of fruitfulness and a multitude of people (35:11; 17:4-6)
the land of Canaan for an everlasting possession (17:8), conveyed to Joseph's sons

schools.summary: Jacob adopting Ephraim and Manasseh as his own: 'and now thy two sons, Ephraim and Manasseh, which were born unto thee in the land of Egypt before I came unto thee into Egypt, are mine; as Reuben and Simeon, they shall be mine' — Jacob's act — 'thy two sons, Ephraim and Manasseh... are mine; as Reuben and Simeon, they shall be mine': Jacob adopting Joseph's two sons as his own, reckoning them as his immediate sons (as Reuben and Simeon, his firstborn and second), each to be the head of a tribe in Israel (the adoption that gave Joseph the double portion of the firstborn, two tribes, Ephraim and Manasseh, where the other sons had but one each, the birthright, forfeited by Reuben, 49:3-4; 1 Chronicles 5:1-2, given to Joseph in this double portion, the elevation of Joseph's sons to the rank of patriarchs of tribes); Ephraim and Manasseh made equal to Reuben and Simeon (Jacob's own eldest sons) — each a tribe-head (the two sons of Joseph counted among the twelve, so that Joseph, through them, had the double portion, the birthright honor, the double portion, Deuteronomy 21:17, given to Joseph); Jacob's adoption (by the Spirit, foreseeing the tribes) — the double portion to Joseph (the beloved son of Rachel, the providence ordering the tribes, Ephraim and Manasseh among the twelve, Levi being set apart for the priesthood, so the number kept, the covenant blessing conveyed, the elevation of Joseph's seed) — Calvin, Gill.

debates.question: What does Jacob's adoption of Ephraim and Manasseh accomplish?

sides.position: It gives Joseph the double portion of the firstborn - his two sons made Jacob's immediate sons, each the head of a tribe (1 Chronicles 5:1-2; Deuteronomy 21:17), the birthright (forfeited by Reuben, 49:3-4) conveyed to the beloved son of Rachel

themes: And now thy two sons, Ephraim and Manasseh... are mine; as Reuben and Simeon, they shall be mine
Jacob adopting Ephraim and Manasseh as his own (the crux of the adoption)
the double portion of the firstborn given to Joseph, his two sons made tribe-heads (1 Chronicles 5:1-2; Deuteronomy 21:17)
the birthright (forfeited by Reuben, 49:3-4) conveyed to the beloved son of Rachel

schools.summary: Joseph's later sons reckoned under Ephraim and Manasseh: 'and thy issue, which thou begettest after them, shall be thine, and shall be called after the name of their brethren in their inheritance' — any later sons of Joseph (born after Ephraim and Manasseh) would be Joseph's own (not adopted as tribe-heads), but reckoned under the tribes of Ephraim and Manasseh in their inheritance (the distinction, only the two firstborn adopted as tribes, the ordering of the inheritance); later sons would be Joseph's (not Jacob's adopted) — counted under Ephraim and Manasseh (the two firstborn alone the tribe-heads, the distinction preserved); 'called after the name of their brethren in their inheritance' (later sons reckoned within the tribes of Ephraim and Manasseh, not separate tribes, the ordering of the inheritance, the providence keeping the number of the tribes, the double portion to Joseph in the two adopted sons) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And thy issue, which thou begettest after them, shall be thine, and shall be called after the name of their brethren in their inheritance
Joseph's later sons reckoned under Ephraim and Manasseh
only the two firstborn adopted as tribe-heads (v.5)
the ordering that kept the double portion to Joseph

schools.summary: Jacob recalling Rachel's death and burial: 'and as for me, when I came from Padan, Rachel died by me in the land of Canaan in the way, when yet there was but a little way to come unto Ephrath: and I buried her there in the way of Ephrath; the same is Bethlehem' — Jacob recalling the death of Rachel (Joseph's mother, 35:16-19) on the way to Ephrath (Bethlehem, the tender memory of the beloved wife, the mention, perhaps explaining why he honored her sons with the double portion, or why he buried her by the way, not in the cave of Machpelah, the recollection of Rachel); Jacob's tender mention of Rachel (the beloved wife, 29:18, 30) — the grief still felt (the love for her that honored her son Joseph, and his sons, vv.5, the memory of the dear wife); 'I buried her there in the way of Ephrath; the same is Bethlehem' (Rachel buried by the way, 35:19-20, near Bethlehem, the place, the fathers note, near where the Messiah would be born, Micah 5:2; Matthew 2:1, the memory of the burial, the tender recollection, and, some note, an apology to Joseph for not burying his mother in Machpelah) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And as for me, when I came from Padan, Rachel died by me in the land of Canaan... and I buried her there in the way of Ephrath; the same is Bethlehem
Jacob recalling Rachel's death and burial
the tender memory of the beloved wife (35:16-19; 29:18, 30)
buried near Bethlehem, where the Messiah would be born (Micah 5:2; Matthew 2:1)

schools.summary: Jacob's dim sight and his question about the lads: 'and Israel beheld Joseph's sons, and said, Who are these?' — Jacob, his eyes dim with age (v.10), seeing the lads but not clearly discerning them (the dimness of sight, as Isaac, 27:1, the question 'Who are these?' not knowing them by sight, the setting for the blessing, vv.9-20); Jacob's question — asking who the lads were (his sight failing, v.10, the inquiry preparing for the blessing, Joseph's answer, v.9, the formal question, perhaps, the witnesses note, to draw out the solemn presentation, as in adoption); Jacob's dim sight (v.10) — the aged patriarch, like Isaac (27:1), not seeing clearly (the contrast, Isaac was deceived by his dim sight, 27:23; Jacob, though dim, blessed by faith and the Spirit, wittingly, v.14, the question that began the blessing) — Henry, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Israel beheld Joseph's sons, and said, Who are these?
Jacob's dim sight and his question about the lads
the aged patriarch, dim of sight like Isaac (27:1; v.10)
yet to bless by faith and the Spirit, wittingly (v.14)

schools.summary: Joseph presenting his sons for blessing: 'and Joseph said unto his father, They are my sons, whom God hath given me in this place. And he said, Bring them, I pray thee, unto me, and I will bless them' — Joseph answering that the lads were his sons, given him by God in Egypt (the godly acknowledgment, the sons God's gift, Psalm 127:3, the presentation for the blessing, Joseph owning God's gift); Joseph's words — the sons 'God hath given me' (the godly view of children, as God's gift, 33:5; Psalm 127:3, the acknowledgment of God, the gift owned even in Egypt); 'Bring them... and I will bless them' (Jacob's request to bring the lads near, that he might bless them, the patriarchal blessing, the conveying of the covenant, the solemn act prepared, the blessing of faith, Hebrews 11:21) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Joseph said unto his father, They are my sons, whom God hath given me in this place... Bring them, I pray thee, unto me, and I will bless them
Joseph presenting his sons for blessing
the sons owned as God's gift (33:5; Psalm 127:3)
brought for the patriarchal blessing of faith (Hebrews 11:21)

schools.summary: Jacob's dim eyes and his embrace of the lads: 'now the eyes of Israel were dim for age, so that he could not see. And he brought them near unto him; and he kissed them, and embraced them' — Jacob's eyes dim with age (he could not see clearly, as Isaac, 27:1, the lads brought near, Jacob kissing and embracing them, the affection of the aged patriarch, the tender scene); Jacob's dim sight (the infirmity of age, 27:1) — like Isaac before him (the dimness, yet, unlike Isaac, Jacob would bless wittingly, not deceived, v.14); 'he kissed them, and embraced them' (Jacob's tender affection for the lads, kissing, embracing, the love of the grandfather, the tenderness preparing for the blessing, vv.11-20) — Gill.

themes: Now the eyes of Israel were dim for age, so that he could not see... and he kissed them, and embraced them
Jacob's dim eyes and his embrace of the lads
the aged patriarch, dim of sight like Isaac (27:1)
the tender affection of the grandfather before the blessing

schools.summary: Jacob marveling at God's mercy beyond his hope: 'and Israel said unto Joseph, I had not thought to see thy face: and, lo, God hath shewed me also thy seed' — Jacob marveling: he had never thought to see Joseph's face again (having mourned him as dead, 37:34-35; 42:36, the wonder of the reunion, beyond all his hope, the mercy of God past expectation); God had shown Jacob not only Joseph (whom he had not thought to see) but 'also thy seed' (his sons) — the mercy beyond expectation (not only the son restored, but his grandsons too, the abundant goodness of God, the overflowing mercy); Jacob's wonder at God's mercy — the goodness of God, who gives beyond our asking or thinking (Ephesians 3:20, the providence crowning the long sorrow with abundant joy, Joseph alive, exalted, and his seed before Jacob, the mercy past hope) — Wesley, Gill.

themes: And Israel said unto Joseph, I had not thought to see thy face: and, lo, God hath shewed me also thy seed
Jacob marveling at God's mercy beyond his hope
the wonder of the reunion past expectation (37:34-35; 42:36)
the abundant goodness of God who gives beyond our thinking (Ephesians 3:20)

schools.summary: Joseph presenting the lads with reverence: 'and Joseph brought them out from between his knees, and he bowed himself with his face to the earth' — Joseph taking the lads from between Jacob's knees (where they had been, near the patriarch) and bowing himself to the earth (in reverence before his father, and for the blessing, the reverence of the great ruler before the aged patriarch, the humble homage, a further fulfillment of the dreams, 37:7-10, Joseph and his bowing before the father); Joseph taking the lads from Jacob's knees (the place of nearness, or of adoption) — to present them rightly for the blessing (vv.13-14, the arranging of the lads); 'he bowed himself with his face to the earth' (Joseph's deep reverence before his father, the great ruler of Egypt humbling himself before the dying patriarch, the honor to the father, Exodus 20:12, the homage for the blessing, the reverence of the godly son) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Joseph brought them out from between his knees, and he bowed himself with his face to the earth
Joseph presenting the lads with reverence
the great ruler humbling himself before the dying patriarch (37:7-10)
the honor to the father, presenting the lads for the blessing (Exodus 20:12)

schools.summary: Joseph arranging the lads for the blessing: 'and Joseph took them both, Ephraim in his right hand toward Israel's left hand, and Manasseh in his left hand toward Israel's right hand, and brought them near unto him' — Joseph carefully placing the lads: Ephraim (the younger) at Jacob's left, Manasseh (the elder) at Jacob's right (the arrangement intended that Jacob's right hand, the hand of the chief blessing, should fall on Manasseh the firstborn, the careful ordering by the right of primogeniture); Joseph's arrangement — Ephraim (younger) to Jacob's left, Manasseh (elder) to Jacob's right (the intent that the elder receive the right-hand blessing, the natural order); 'brought them near unto him' (Joseph bringing the lads so arranged, for the blessing, the design that Manasseh, the firstborn, receive the right hand, the order that Jacob would cross, v.14, guiding his hands wittingly to prefer Ephraim) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Joseph took them both, Ephraim in his right hand toward Israel's left hand, and Manasseh in his left hand toward Israel's right hand, and brought them near unto him
Joseph arranging the lads for the blessing
Ephraim to Jacob's left, Manasseh to his right - the elder to receive the right-hand blessing
the order by primogeniture that Jacob would cross (v.14)

schools.summary: Jacob crossing his hands to prefer Ephraim: 'and Israel stretched out his right hand, and laid it upon Ephraim's head, who was the younger, and his left hand upon Manasseh's head, guiding his hands wittingly; for Manasseh was the firstborn' — Jacob crossing his arms (or hands), laying his right hand (the hand of the chief blessing) on Ephraim the younger, and his left on Manasseh the elder (reversing Joseph's arrangement, v.13, the crossing of the hands, the preferring of the younger); 'guiding his hands wittingly' — Jacob doing this knowingly, deliberately (not by mistake, as his dim eyes might have caused, v.10, but 'wittingly,' well knowing what he did, the crossing was no error but a deliberate, Spirit-led act, Jacob, blessing by faith, Hebrews 11:21, and prophetic foresight, deliberately preferring Ephraim, the younger, over Manasseh, the elder, the sovereign election of the younger, as God had chosen Jacob over Esau, 25:23; Isaac over Ishmael; Abel over Cain; the grace that often passes by the elder for the younger, contrary to nature's order, that the election might be of grace, not of birth, Romans 9:11-13, the prophetic preferring, foreseeing Ephraim's greater future, v.19); 'for Manasseh was the firstborn' (the note that Manasseh was the elder, yet the younger preferred, the reversal of the natural order, the sovereign grace, the pattern of election, the younger preferred, the act of faith, Hebrews 11:21, 'by faith Jacob blessed both the sons of Joseph') — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

debates.question: Why did Jacob cross his hands to put the right on Ephraim the younger?

sides.position: No error of dim sight, but a deliberate, Spirit-led, prophetic act ('wittingly') - Jacob by faith (Hebrews 11:21) preferring the younger over the elder, as God chose Jacob over Esau (25:23), Isaac over Ishmael; the sovereign election of grace over nature's order (Romans 9:11-13), foreseeing Ephraim's greater future (v.19)

themes: And Israel stretched out his right hand, and laid it upon Ephraim's head, who was the younger... guiding his hands wittingly; for Manasseh was the firstborn
Jacob crossing his hands to prefer Ephraim (the crux of the sovereign election)
no error of dim sight, but a deliberate, Spirit-led act ('wittingly'), by faith (Hebrews 11:21)
the sovereign election of the younger over the elder, of grace over nature (25:23; Romans 9:11-13)

schools.summary: Jacob blessing by the covenant God who shepherded him: 'and he blessed Joseph, and said, God, before whom my fathers Abraham and Isaac did walk, the God which fed me all my life long unto this day' — Jacob blessing (Joseph, in his sons) invoking the covenant God, before whom Abraham and Isaac had walked (in faith and obedience, 17:1; 24:40, the God of the fathers, the covenant God invoked in the blessing); the tender phrase — 'the God which fed me (Hebrew, shepherded me) all my life long unto this day': Jacob owning God as his shepherd, who had fed and led him all his life (the Shepherd of Israel, Psalm 23:1; 80:1, the God who had kept him through all his wanderings and troubles, the grateful testimony of the aged saint to God's lifelong care, the first calling of God a Shepherd, a name dear to the church, John 10:11); Jacob's blessing (invoking the covenant, shepherding God) — the faith that owned God's lifelong faithfulness (the blessing of the dying patriarch, by faith, Hebrews 11:21, the covenant conveyed through the God of the fathers and the Shepherd of his life) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley, Calvin.

themes: And he blessed Joseph, and said, God, before whom my fathers Abraham and Isaac did walk, the God which fed me all my life long unto this day
Jacob blessing by the covenant God who shepherded him
the God of the fathers, before whom Abraham and Isaac walked (17:1)
the God who shepherded him all his life long, the first calling of God a Shepherd (Psalm 23:1; John 10:11)

schools.summary: The Angel-Redeemer invoked in the blessing: 'the Angel which redeemed me from all evil, bless the lads; and let my name be named on them, and the name of my fathers Abraham and Isaac; and let them grow into a multitude in the midst of the earth' — the great phrase — 'The Angel which redeemed me from all evil, bless the lads': Jacob invoking 'the Angel' who had redeemed him from all evil (the witnesses identifying this Angel — not a created angel, but the Angel of the LORD, the Angel of God's presence, Exodus 23:20-21; Isaiah 63:9, the same with the God invoked in v.15, the blessing being one, addressed to God and the Angel as one, the divine Angel, the Angel of the covenant, Malachi 3:1, whom many of the fathers and the Reformed identify as the pre-incarnate Christ, the second Person, the Angel-Jehovah who appeared to the patriarchs, 16:7-13; 22:11-18; 31:11-13; 32:24-30, the proof: Jacob asks this Angel to 'bless the lads,' a divine act, prayer addressed to him as God, the 'Angel' and 'God,' v.15, joined as one in the blessing); 'redeemed' (Hebrew goel, the kinsman-redeemer) — the first use of 'redeem' in Scripture (the Angel who had redeemed, delivered, rescued, Jacob from all evil, from Esau, Laban, all his troubles, the Redeemer, a name pointing to Christ, the Goel, the Kinsman-Redeemer, Job 19:25, the great Redeemer from all evil, sin, and death, the testimony of the aged saint to his Redeemer); 'bless the lads; and let my name be named on them, and the name of my fathers... and let them grow into a multitude' (the blessing proper: that the Angel bless the lads, that Jacob's name, and Abraham's and Isaac's, be named on them, they reckoned among the covenant seed, the heirs of the promise, and that they grow into a multitude, the great increase, fulfilled, Numbers 1; Ephraim and Manasseh great tribes, the covenant blessing conveyed, the increase promised) — Calvin, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: Who is 'the Angel which redeemed me from all evil'?

sides.position: Not a created angel, but the Angel of the LORD, the Angel of the covenant, joined as one with the God of v.15 (asked to 'bless,' a divine act) - whom many of the fathers and the Reformed identify as the pre-incarnate Christ (Exodus 23:20-21; Malachi 3:1); 'redeemed' (goel) the first use of the word, the Redeemer pointing to Christ the Kinsman-Redeemer (Job 19:25)

themes: The Angel which redeemed me from all evil, bless the lads; and let my name be named on them, and the name of my fathers Abraham and Isaac; and let them grow into a multitude in the midst of the earth
the Angel-Redeemer invoked in the blessing (the crux of the redeeming Angel)
not a created angel, but the Angel of the covenant, joined as one with God (asked to 'bless') - the pre-incarnate Christ (Exodus 23:20-21; Malachi 3:1)
'redeemed' (goel) the first use of the word, the Redeemer pointing to Christ the Kinsman-Redeemer (Job 19:25)

schools.summary: Joseph displeased and trying to redirect his father's hand: 'and when Joseph saw that his father laid his right hand upon the head of Ephraim, it displeased him: and he held up his father's hand, to remove it from Ephraim's head unto Manasseh's head' — Joseph, seeing the right hand (the chief blessing) on Ephraim the younger (not Manasseh the elder, as he had arranged, v.13), displeased (the natural concern, that the firstborn should have the chief blessing, the supposing it an error of Jacob's dim sight, v.10); Joseph's displeasure — supposing Jacob mistaken (by his dim eyes, the natural regard for the firstborn's right, the misunderstanding, not yet knowing Jacob acted wittingly and prophetically, v.14); 'he held up his father's hand, to remove it' (Joseph trying to redirect Jacob's right hand to Manasseh, the attempt to correct the supposed error, the natural effort, to secure the firstborn's blessing, the attempt Jacob would refuse, vv.18-19) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: And when Joseph saw that his father laid his right hand upon the head of Ephraim, it displeased him: and he held up his father's hand, to remove it
Joseph displeased and trying to redirect his father's hand
the natural regard for the firstborn's right (v.13)
supposing an error of dim sight, not knowing Jacob acted wittingly (v.14)

schools.summary: Joseph urging the right hand on the firstborn: 'and Joseph said unto his father, Not so, my father: for this is the firstborn; put thy right hand upon his head' — Joseph urging his father: 'Not so' — Manasseh was the firstborn, and should have the right hand (the chief blessing, the plea for the firstborn's right, the natural insistence, supposing Jacob mistaken, v.17, the correction urged); Joseph's appeal — Manasseh the firstborn (the right of primogeniture, the natural claim, the plea that the elder receive the chief blessing); 'put thy right hand upon his head' (Joseph urging the right hand to Manasseh, the insistence on the firstborn's blessing, the correction Jacob would refuse, v.19, 'I know it, my son,' the natural order pressed) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke.

themes: And Joseph said unto his father, Not so, my father: for this is the firstborn; put thy right hand upon his head
Joseph urging the right hand on the firstborn
the natural insistence on Manasseh's right of primogeniture
the correction Jacob would refuse (v.19)

schools.summary: Jacob foretelling Ephraim's greater future: 'and his father refused, and said, I know it, my son, I know it: he also shall become a people, and he also shall be great: but truly his younger brother shall be greater than he, and his seed shall become a multitude of nations' — Jacob refusing to move his hand, affirming twice 'I know it' — he knew Manasseh was the firstborn (v.14), and acted not by error but by deliberate, prophetic intent (the confirmation that the crossing was wittingly done, v.14, the firm refusal); Jacob's prophecy — 'he also shall become a people... but truly his younger brother shall be greater... his seed shall become a multitude of nations': Jacob foretelling that both would become peoples (Manasseh great too), but that Ephraim (the younger) would be the greater, his seed a 'multitude of nations' (literally, 'fulness of the nations,' the prophecy fulfilled — Ephraim became the larger and leading tribe, the chief of the ten northern tribes, often called by Ephraim's name, Hosea, Isaiah, Ephraim more numerous, Numbers 1:33, 35, though by Manasseh's later growth the matter varied, the sovereign election of the younger, the grace that prefers the lesser, as Jacob over Esau, 25:23, the prophetic foresight, the divine ordering of the tribes); Jacob's foretelling (by the Spirit) Ephraim's greatness — the prophecy of the younger's preeminence (the providence ordering the tribes, the election of grace, the younger over the elder, contrary to nature, that the choice might be God's) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

debates.question: What does Jacob's preferring of Ephraim foretell?

sides.position: Both would be peoples, but the younger Ephraim the greater (fulfilled in Ephraim's leading the northern tribes, Numbers 1:33, 35) - the sovereign election of the younger over the elder (as Jacob over Esau, 25:23), the prophetic foresight ordering the tribes, the grace that prefers the lesser

themes: And his father refused, and said, I know it, my son, I know it... but truly his younger brother shall be greater than he, and his seed shall become a multitude of nations
Jacob foretelling Ephraim's greater future (the crux of the younger preferred)
the deliberate, prophetic act ('I know it') - Ephraim the younger to be greater, fulfilled in his leading the northern tribes
the sovereign election of the younger over the elder (as Jacob over Esau, 25:23), the grace that prefers the lesser

schools.summary: Jacob making the lads a pattern of blessing, Ephraim first: 'and he blessed them that day, saying, In thee shall Israel bless, saying, God make thee as Ephraim and as Manasseh: and he set Ephraim before Manasseh' — Jacob making Ephraim and Manasseh a proverb of blessing in Israel: that the people would bless their children with 'God make thee as Ephraim and as Manasseh' (a form of blessing, naming the two as the pattern of the blessed, the honor put on the lads, the form of blessing in Israel, used to this day among the Jews); the form of blessing (Ephraim first, the preferred) — the two made the pattern of the blessed in Israel (the high honor, the proverb of blessing); 'he set Ephraim before Manasseh' (Jacob naming Ephraim first, the younger before the elder, the confirmation of the preferring, vv.14, 19, the order of the tribes set, Ephraim first, the sovereign election sealed in the form of blessing) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And he blessed them that day, saying, In thee shall Israel bless, saying, God make thee as Ephraim and as Manasseh: and he set Ephraim before Manasseh
Jacob making the lads a pattern of blessing, Ephraim first
the two made the proverb of blessing in Israel (used to this day)
Ephraim set first, the sovereign election sealed (vv.14, 19)

schools.summary: Jacob's dying assurance of the return to Canaan: 'and Israel said unto Joseph, Behold, I die: but God shall be with you, and bring you again unto the land of your fathers' — Jacob announcing his approaching death (the dying patriarch, the solemn farewell, the faith that faced death calmly, his hope in the covenant promise, the dying testimony); Jacob's assurance — 'God shall be with you' (the covenant God's presence with Israel in Egypt, as promised, 46:4, the comfort to Joseph and his seed, the faith that God would not forsake them); 'bring you again unto the land of your fathers' (Jacob's dying faith in the return to Canaan, the Exodus, foretold, 15:13-16; 46:4, the assurance that Egypt was not their home, but Canaan, the covenant land, the faith that looked beyond the present sojourn to the promised inheritance, Hebrews 11:13-16, 22, the dying patriarch's hope, echoed in Joseph's own dying charge, 50:24-25) — Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Israel said unto Joseph, Behold, I die: but God shall be with you, and bring you again unto the land of your fathers
Jacob's dying assurance of the return to Canaan
the covenant God's presence with Israel in Egypt (46:4)
the dying faith in the return to Canaan, Egypt not their home (15:13-16; 50:24; Hebrews 11:22)

schools.summary: Jacob giving Joseph the portion of Shechem: 'moreover I have given to thee one portion above thy brethren, which I took out of the hand of the Amorite with my sword and with my bow' — Jacob giving Joseph one portion (Hebrew shechem, a play on the place Shechem) above his brethren (the special gift, the double portion of the firstborn, in his sons, vv.5, and this portion of land, the honor to Joseph, the beloved son, the portion above the brethren); 'one portion' (shechem) — likely the parcel of ground at Shechem (33:19, bought by Jacob, where Joseph would be buried, Joshua 24:32, the special inheritance, the gift to Joseph, the word a play on Shechem); the phrase 'which I took out of the hand of the Amorite with my sword and with my bow' variously understood — (a) the parcel at Shechem (bought, 33:19, but perhaps later seized or recovered from the Amorites, or defended by the sword after the slaughter, ch. 34); (b) a prophetic gift (Jacob, by faith, giving in prospect the land the seed would take from the Amorites by sword and bow, the conquest, Joshua); (c) 'sword and bow' as Jacob's prayer and faith (the Targum: 'my prayer and supplication,' the witnesses agreeing the portion was Joseph's special inheritance, the Shechem parcel, where his bones were laid, Joshua 24:32, given by the dying patriarch) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

debates.question: What is the 'one portion... taken from the Amorite with my sword and bow'?

sides.position: The parcel of ground at Shechem (bought, 33:19; where Joseph's bones were laid, Joshua 24:32) - perhaps recovered or defended from the Amorites by the sword (ch. 34), or given prophetically in prospect of the conquest
'Sword and bow' taken as Jacob's prayer and supplication (the Targum's paraphrase), the portion won by faith and prayer

themes: Moreover I have given to thee one portion above thy brethren, which I took out of the hand of the Amorite with my sword and with my bow
Jacob giving Joseph the portion of Shechem
the special inheritance (shechem), the parcel where Joseph's bones would be laid (33:19; Joshua 24:32)
taken from the Amorite by sword and bow (ch. 34), or by Jacob's prayer (the Targum)
